schools.summary: Gill: they turned from Kadesh, and so turned their backs on the land of Canaan, on whose borders they had been.
Henry: a short account of a long stay — they compassed Mount Seir nearly thirty-eight years, wandering in the deserts of Seir.

themes: we compassed mount Seir many days
nearly thirty-eight years of wandering
unbelief lengthening the way

schools.summary: Gill: this was while about Mount Seir.
Keil & Delitzsch: the command to turn northward, round the southern end of Seir, without warring with Edom.

themes: the LORD spake unto me
God's word ending the long delay
his people moving at his word

schools.summary: Gill: it was time to be gone from thence, as from Horeb.
Keil & Delitzsch: turn northward, the people to pass the coast of their brethren the children of Esau.

themes: Ye have compassed this mountain long enough: turn you northward
God's measure set to the trial
the discipline timed, sending them forward

schools.summary: Gill: a strict charge given to the people.
Ellicott: said in view of the request to pass through (Num. 20:17), Edom being their brethren, the children of Esau, who would be afraid of them.

themes: Ye are to pass through the coast of your brethren the children of Esau... take ye good heed
careful self-restraint toward kinsmen
power held in check by reverence and right

schools.summary: Clarke: 'meddle not with them,' that is, the Edomites.
Ellicott: Esau's inheritance was won earlier than Jacob's, as Ishmael's development preceded Isaac's (Gen. 25:16).

themes: I have given mount Seir unto Esau for a possession
God's sovereign disposal of the nations' lands
respecting the bounds he set for others (Deut. 32:8)

schools.summary: Gill: though they had manna daily, they might buy other food if they would, but were not to take it by force or stealth — they were able to pay; they were to buy water also for money.

themes: Ye shall buy meat of them for money... and... buy water of them for money
honest dealing, not seizing
power no licence for plunder (Rom. 12:17)

schools.summary: Gill: God had increased their cattle and substance, even in a wilderness.
Clarke: God had given them much property, gold and silver to buy what they needed, so he would not permit them to plunder.

themes: these forty years the LORD thy God hath been with thee; thou hast lacked nothing
God's faithful provision in the wilderness
seeking first his kingdom, they shall not lack (Matt. 6:33)

schools.summary: Gill: they passed along the coasts and borders of Esau's country.
Keil & Delitzsch: passing by Edom through the way of the plain from Elath and Eziongaber, then turning to the wilderness of Moab.

themes: we passed by from our brethren the children of Esau... and passed by the way of the wilderness of Moab
the command obediently kept
the march ordered by God's word

schools.summary: Gill: this was spoken when Israel was upon the borders of Moab.
Keil & Delitzsch: distress not Moab, for Ar was given to the children of Lot for a possession.

themes: Distress not the Moabites... I have given Ar unto the children of Lot for a possession
God's faithfulness to Lot's line
his sovereign disposal of the nations' lands

schools.summary: Gill: the Emims are read of as early as the times of Chedorlaomer, dwelling in Kirjathaim, a city later given to Reuben; a people great, many, and tall as the Anakims.

themes: The Emims dwelt therein in times past, a people great, and many, and tall, as the Anakims
God dispossessing even giants
he could clear the Anakim before Israel

schools.summary: Gill: they were reckoned Rephaim, a name for giants in early times, even as the Anakims were.
Clarke: the word is not 'giants' but Rephaim, a particular people.

themes: Which also were accounted giants, as the Anakims; but the Moabites call them Emims
peoples of great might dispossessed
no strength standing when God removes a nation

schools.summary: Gill: Seir was named from Seir the Horite who dwelt there before Esau; the Horim seem so called from dwelling in holes and caves; the children of Esau succeeded them, as Israel did the land the LORD gave.

themes: the children of Esau succeeded them... as Israel did unto the land of his possession
nations dispossessed a pledge of Israel's inheritance
the conquest on the sure ground of divine gift

schools.summary: Gill: it is called the valley of Zered, the word meaning both valley and brook, near Dibongad; they rose and went over as commanded.

themes: get you over the brook Zered. And we went over the brook Zered
a marked boundary of the journey crossed
the people pressing on by God's appointed stages

schools.summary: Gill: the space — the number of the days, or years.
Keil & Delitzsch: thirty-eight years until all the generation of the men of war were wasted out, as the LORD sware.

themes: the space... was thirty and eight years; until all the generation of the men of war were wasted out
God's oath against unbelief fulfilled
the carcasses fallen in the wilderness (Heb. 3:17)

schools.summary: Gill: God's power was exerted in wrath and vengeance for their murmuring at the report of the spies; strong is his hand, and when lifted up it falls heavy, smiting them with one disease or another.

themes: the hand of the LORD was against them, to destroy them... until they were consumed
the active judgment of God on unbelief
none standing when his hand is lifted in wrath

schools.summary: Gill: they were consumed by wasting diseases and judgments of one kind or another; the old generation was now wholly gone from among the people.

themes: when all the men of war were consumed and dead from among the people
the old generation wholly passed away
the promise carried on to the children

schools.summary: Gill: this was at the brook Zered, having passed it, or at Dibongad, their next station.
Keil & Delitzsch: the word to pass through Ar, the coast of Moab, and to distress not Ammon.

themes: the LORD spake unto me
God's renewed word after the judgment
leading the new generation on

schools.summary: Gill: this means passing over the river Arnon, by the city Ar of Moab situated by it.
Ellicott: Ar is Rabbath-Moab, the present ruin Rabba.

themes: Thou art to pass over through Ar, the coast of Moab, this day
the steady advance ordered by God
the people pressing on toward the land

schools.summary: Gill: the Ammonites dwelt near the Moabites and were brethren, both descending from Lot. Calvin: God now makes provision for the Ammonites, their condition the same as the Moabites', being descended from Lot.

themes: distress them not... I have given it unto the children of Lot for a possession
God's faithfulness to Lot's seed
his sovereign disposal apportioning each people its bounds (Acts 17:26)

schools.summary: Gill: Ammon was reckoned a land of giants, as Moab was.
Clarke: the Zamzummims are the same as the Zuzim (Gen. 14:5), of whom little is known.

themes: That also was accounted a land of giants... the Ammonites call them Zamzummims
giants dispossessed before Ammon
God, not human strength, settling the nations

schools.summary: Gill: the Lord destroyed the Zamzummims before the children of Ammon, who would otherwise have been too much for them, being so numerous and of gigantic stature.
Ellicott: the conquest of Canaan is here, as it were, begun, God destroying nations before others.

themes: the LORD destroyed them before them; and they succeeded them
God dispossessing giants for Ammon
no greatness standing when God fights

schools.summary: Gill: God did the like for the children of Esau as for the Ammonites; they succeeded the Horims and dwelt there to this day.

themes: he destroyed the Horims from before them; and they succeeded them
God's hand in every settlement of the nations
a pledge of the like for Israel

schools.summary: Gill: the Avims are the same as the Avites, who dwelt in part of the land of the Philistines, where Azzah (Gaza) was; Hazerim signifies courts; the Caphtorims came forth out of Caphtor and destroyed them.

themes: the Caphtorims... destroyed them, and dwelt in their stead
one heathen nation displacing another
God setting the bounds of every habitation (Acts 17:26)

schools.summary: Gill: Arnon was on the border of Moab, dividing between Moab and the Amorites.
Keil & Delitzsch: I have given Sihon and his land into thine hand; begin to possess it.

themes: I have given into thine hand Sihon the Amorite... begin to possess it
the conquest begun on God's promise
the victory assured by his word before the battle

schools.summary: Gill: this fulfilled the prophecies Moses had delivered (Exod. 15:14-16).
Keil & Delitzsch: the dread and fear of Israel put upon the nations under the whole heaven.

themes: This day will I begin to put the dread of thee... upon the nations
God striking terror before his people
the enemies' hearts melting, the battle the LORD's (Josh. 2:9)

schools.summary: Gill: Kedemoth was a city later given to the Levites, near a wilderness named from it.
Keil & Delitzsch: messengers sent to Sihon with words of peace.

themes: I sent messengers... unto Sihon king of Heshbon with words of peace
peace offered before war
the guilt of conflict on the one who refused (Deut. 20:10)

schools.summary: Gill: refers to the like request made before (Num. 21:22).
Keil & Delitzsch: to keep the high way, neither turning right nor left.

themes: Let me pass through thy land: I will go along by the high way
a fair and harmless request
leaving Sihon no just cause for war

schools.summary: Gill: they desired only to pay for provision, if Sihon thought fit to give it.
Wesley: 'on my feet,' or with a company on foot, added significantly, since a host of horsemen might have done more mischief.

themes: Thou shalt sell me meat for money... only I will pass through on my feet
terms studiously inoffensive
heightening the guilt of Sihon's refusal

schools.summary: Gill, with Jarchi: this respects not passing through their land (which neither granted) but the buying of food and drink; their aim was only to pass over Jordan into the land God gave them.

themes: as the children of Esau... and the Moabites... did unto me; until I shall pass over Jordan
citing precedent to clear themselves
seeking passage, not conquest

schools.summary: Clarke: the hardening, as with Pharaoh (Exod. 4:21).
Ellicott: Jehovah gave Sihon his strength, as he had done Pharaoh, the obstinacy serving God's purpose of judgment.

themes: the LORD thy God hardened his spirit, and made his heart obstinate, that he might deliver him into thy hand
God's sovereignty over the wicked
hardening whom he will, for judgment (Rom. 9:18)

schools.summary: Gill: this was spoken about the time the messengers had returned with Sihon's answer.
Ellicott: in all Israel's conquests Jehovah gave the order to begin the attack (Deut. 7:2).

themes: I have begun to give Sihon and his land before thee: begin to possess
the conquest God's gift and commission
Israel moving at his sovereign word (Deut. 7:2)

schools.summary: Gill: Sihon, perceiving Israel upon the march toward his land, gathered all his people and went out of Heshbon his capital.
Keil & Delitzsch: the defeat of Sihon, as in Num. 21:23-26.

themes: Then Sihon came out against us, he and all his people, to fight at Jahaz
the hardened aggressor walking into judgment
his fury overruled to his own ruin

schools.summary: Gill: the LORD delivered him with their lands before them.
Keil & Delitzsch: Israel smote Sihon, his sons, and all his people.

themes: the LORD our God delivered him before us; and we smote him, and his sons, and all his people
the victory God's gift, not Israel's strength
the promise fulfilled in fact

schools.summary: Gill: they took all his cities, Heshbon and others.
Ellicott: 'utterly destroyed' means devoted to destruction, made cherem like the spoil of Jericho — this could only be by divine direction.

themes: we took all his cities... and utterly destroyed... we left none to remain
the cherem ordered by God, not human cruelty
judgment on the Amorite whose iniquity was full (Gen. 15:16)

schools.summary: Gill: these they did not destroy but preserved alive for their own use and profit.
Keil & Delitzsch: the cattle and the spoil of the cities taken as prey.

themes: Only the cattle we took for a prey unto ourselves, and the spoil of the cities
the precise measure of the sentence
God punishing the Amorite and providing for his people

schools.summary: Gill: Aroer was by the brink of Arnon, on the border of Moab, the principal city.
Clarke: refers to the river of Arnon (Num. 21:13).

themes: there was not one city too strong for us: the LORD our God delivered all unto us
every stronghold falling at God's giving
no defence of man standing against him

schools.summary: Gill: the land of Ammon was then in their possession, and Israel came not unto any place of the river Jabbok, the border of Ammon, nor whatever God forbade.

themes: Only unto the land of the children of Ammon thou camest not... nor... whatsoever the LORD our God forbad us
obedience amid victory
taking only what God gave, sparing what he reserved
